statusLine: EU fuel supplies are under strain with 17 of 27 countries facing critical shortages in at least one fuel type, despite average reserves remaining above mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security is under significant pressure following months of Middle East supply disruptions and strategic reserve releases. While EU-wide averages remain above the mandatory 90-day minimum at 112.8 days for petrol, 101.5 days for diesel, and 95.1 days for jet fuel, the distribution is highly uneven across member states.

A concerning 17 of 27 EU countries are experiencing critical shortages in at least one fuel category, with Romania showing the most severe depletion across all three fuels (40.4 days petrol, 30.2 days diesel, 76.4 days jet fuel). Poland, Luxembourg, and several other countries face similarly dire situations. Jet fuel stocks are particularly vulnerable, with 16 countries below safe levels and 12 in critical status.

The fuel price landscape reflects supply tensions, with Brent crude at $109.26 per barrel—a 50.7% increase since late February. Average EU petrol prices have reached €1.82 per liter, with significant regional variations from Malta's €1.34 to the Netherlands' €2.34. The combination of depleted reserves and elevated prices indicates the EU remains vulnerable to further supply shocks, particularly affecting countries already operating with minimal buffer stocks.
keyPoints: 17 of 27 EU countries face critical shortages in at least one fuel type, despite EU averages above 90-day minimums
Romania shows the most severe depletion with petrol at just 40.4 days and diesel at 30.2 days—well below emergency thresholds
Jet fuel stocks are most vulnerable with 16 countries below safe levels and 12 in critical status
Brent crude oil has surged 50.7% since late February to $109.26/barrel, driving average EU petrol prices to €1.82/liter
Price disparities across the EU are significant, ranging from Malta's €1.34/liter to Netherlands' €2.34/liter for petrol
